standards.title: Logical and Physical Access Security Software, Infrastructure, and Architectures
standards.description: The entity implements logical access security software, infrastructure, and architectures over protected information assets to protect them from security events. This includes implementing access control mechanisms that restrict access to information assets based on the principle of least privilege, deploying authentication mechanisms appropriate to the sensitivity of the systems (including multi-factor authentication for sensitive systems and remote access), maintaining access control lists and role definitions, and implementing boundary protection through firewalls, proxies, and network segmentation. The entity uses a combination of technical and procedural controls to ensure that only authorized individuals and systems can access protected resources.

standards.title: User Registration, Authorization, and Account Provisioning
standards.description: Prior to issuing system credentials and granting system access, the entity registers and authorizes new internal and external users. The registration process includes verification of the user's identity, authorization from appropriate management, and documentation of the access granted. For employees, provisioning is integrated with the HR onboarding process. For contractors and external users, provisioning requires a formal access request approved by the system or data owner. The entity maintains a record of all active users and their authorized access. User access is reviewed periodically (at least annually for standard users, quarterly for privileged users) and access that is no longer required is promptly revoked. The deprovisioning process ensures that access is removed within 24 hours of termination.

standards.title: Role-Based Access and Least Privilege
standards.description: The entity authorizes, modifies, or removes access to data, software, functions, and other protected information assets based on roles, responsibilities, or the system design and changes, giving consideration to the concepts of least privilege and segregation of duties. Access authorizations are aligned with job responsibilities and are updated when roles change. The entity implements role-based access control (RBAC) with clearly defined roles that map to specific sets of permissions. Segregation of duties is enforced to prevent any single individual from having the ability to perform conflicting functions (e.g., the person who approves access cannot be the person who provisions it). Privileged access is restricted to designated personnel and is subject to enhanced monitoring and more frequent review.

standards.title: Physical Access Restriction
standards.description: The entity restricts physical access to facilities and protected information assets to authorized personnel to meet the entity's objectives. Physical access controls include badge-based entry systems, visitor management procedures, video surveillance of entry points, and environmental controls for data center facilities. Physical access authorizations are reviewed periodically and revoked when no longer required. In the IAM context, physical access control systems shall be integrated with the identity management infrastructure so that badge deactivation occurs automatically when an employee's logical access is revoked. Physical access logs shall be maintained and correlated with logical access logs to detect anomalies such as a user authenticating remotely while their badge shows no physical entry to the building.

standards.title: Disposal of Protected Information Assets
standards.description: The entity discontinues logical and physical protections over physical assets only after the ability to read or recover data and software from those assets has been diminished and is no longer required. In the IAM context, this control requires that when identity infrastructure components are decommissioned (directory servers, identity databases, certificate authorities, hardware security modules), all identity data is securely erased or destroyed. Cryptographic keys stored on decommissioned hardware SHALL be destroyed using approved methods. Backup media containing identity data SHALL be tracked and destroyed according to the retention schedule. Authentication logs on decommissioned systems SHALL be archived or destroyed according to the organization's log retention policy before the system is disposed of.

standards.title: Logical Access Security Measures Against External Threats
standards.description: The entity implements logical access security measures to protect against threats from sources outside its system boundaries. These measures include implementing boundary protection (firewalls, web application firewalls, API gateways), requiring multi-factor authentication for all external access, encrypting all data in transit using TLS 1.2+, monitoring external access points for anomalous activity, and maintaining allow-lists for authorized external connections. For identity specifically, the entity SHALL protect externally-facing authentication endpoints from credential-based attacks (brute force, credential stuffing, password spraying) through rate limiting, CAPTCHA, and IP reputation checking. Federation endpoints SHALL validate all incoming assertions and SHALL reject assertions from untrusted identity providers.

standards.title: Restriction and Management of Transmission, Movement, and Removal of Information
standards.description: The entity restricts the transmission, movement, and removal of information to authorized internal and external users and processes, and protects it during transmission, movement, or removal to meet the entity's objectives. In the IAM context, this requires that identity data (user records, credentials, authentication tokens, directory exports) be protected whenever it is transmitted between systems. SCIM provisioning traffic SHALL be encrypted and authenticated. SAML assertions and OIDC tokens SHALL be signed and encrypted. Bulk export of user data SHALL require explicit authorization and SHALL be logged. Mobile device management SHALL prevent unauthorized removal of identity data from managed devices. Data loss prevention (DLP) controls SHALL monitor for and prevent unauthorized transmission of credential data, directory exports, and authentication configuration data.

standards.title: Prevention and Detection of Unauthorized or Malicious Software
standards.description: The entity implements controls to prevent or detect and act upon the introduction of unauthorized or malicious software to meet the entity's objectives. In the IAM context, this control is critical because malware is a primary vector for credential theft (keyloggers, credential harvesting trojans, infostealers). The entity SHALL deploy endpoint protection on all systems that process authentication credentials. Endpoint detection and response (EDR) solutions SHALL monitor for credential dumping tools (mimikatz, secretsdump), unauthorized access to credential stores (SAM database, LSASS process, browser password stores), and suspicious authentication-related processes. Application whitelisting SHALL be implemented on identity infrastructure servers (domain controllers, certificate authorities, identity providers) to prevent execution of unauthorized software that could compromise the identity infrastructure.

standards.title: Risk Assessment of Identity and Access Controls
standards.description: The entity identifies and assesses risks related to the achievement of its objectives, including risks related to the use of information technology. For IAM, this requires regular risk assessment of identity infrastructure including evaluation of the risk of credential compromise, assessment of the adequacy of current authentication mechanisms against emerging threats (phishing, adversary-in-the-middle, session hijacking), identification of excessive or inappropriate access rights through access reviews, evaluation of the risk posed by third-party identity integrations, and assessment of the impact of identity system failures on business operations. Risk assessment results SHALL inform the selection and implementation of identity controls and SHALL be reviewed at least annually or upon significant changes to the identity landscape.

standards.title: Monitoring of Identity and Access Systems
standards.description: The entity monitors system components and the operation of those components for anomalies that are indicative of malicious acts, natural disasters, and errors affecting the entity's ability to meet its objectives; anomalies are analyzed to determine whether they represent security events. For IAM, monitoring SHALL include real-time monitoring of authentication events for patterns indicative of attacks (brute force, credential stuffing, password spraying, token replay), monitoring of privilege escalation and administrative actions, alerting on impossible travel (authentication from geographically distant locations within a short time), detection of anomalous service account behavior, and monitoring of identity infrastructure health (directory replication, certificate expiry, federation endpoint availability). Security events SHALL be escalated through defined incident response procedures.

standards.title: Change Management for Identity Systems
standards.description: The entity authorizes, designs, develops or acquires, configures, documents, tests, approves, and implements changes to infrastructure, data, software, and procedures to meet its objectives. For IAM systems, change management is particularly critical because misconfiguration of identity infrastructure can create security vulnerabilities affecting all connected systems. Changes to authentication policies, access control configurations, federation trust relationships, identity provider settings, and directory schemas SHALL follow the organization's change management process including impact assessment, testing in a non-production environment, approval from the security team, rollback planning, and post-implementation verification. Emergency changes to IAM systems (e.g., emergency MFA policy changes during an incident) SHALL follow an expedited process with retrospective review.

standards.title: Third-Party Identity and Access Risk Management
standards.description: The entity assesses and manages risks associated with vendors and business partners. For IAM, third-party risk management includes evaluating the identity and access management practices of service providers who process organizational data or have access to organizational systems. The entity SHALL assess each third party's authentication standards (MFA enforcement, password policies), access governance processes (provisioning, review, deprovisioning), incident response capabilities for identity compromises, compliance with relevant standards (SOC 2, ISO 27001), and security monitoring of their identity infrastructure. Contracts SHALL specify IAM requirements including MFA enforcement, credential management standards, breach notification timelines for identity-related incidents, and the organization's right to audit the third party's IAM controls. Federated access SHALL be preferred over shared credentials for third-party access.

standards.title: Board Oversight of Identity and Access Controls
standards.description: The board of directors demonstrates independence from management and exercises oversight of the development and performance of internal control. For IAM, this requires that the board or a designated committee receives regular reporting on the effectiveness of identity and access controls, including metrics such as MFA adoption rates, access review completion rates, mean time to deprovision terminated users, number of orphaned accounts discovered, privileged account inventory size and growth trends, and the results of identity-related security incidents. Board oversight ensures that IAM receives appropriate executive attention and funding, and that identity risks are considered as part of the organization's overall risk management strategy. This governance structure is essential for demonstrating to auditors that IAM controls are taken seriously at the highest levels of the organization.

standards.title: Identity Fraud Risk Assessment
standards.description: The entity considers the potential for fraud in assessing risks to the achievement of objectives. For IAM, fraud risk assessment SHALL evaluate the risk of credential fraud (stolen, purchased, or forged credentials), social engineering attacks targeting identity processes (pretexting calls to helpdesks for password resets, deepfake attacks on video-based identity proofing), insider threats from users with excessive privileges, collusion between users to circumvent separation of duties, and manipulation of identity governance processes (fraudulent access requests, manipulation of approval workflows). The entity SHALL implement controls to mitigate identified fraud risks including multi-person approval for high-risk access changes, anomaly detection for identity lifecycle events, and regular reconciliation of actual system access against approved access records.
